ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper proposes a notable and coherent new perspective—composing learned modifier property-profile distributions to populate arbitrarily fine-grained IsA classes—which, according to the summary, is the first application of noun-compound interpretation to downstream IsA extraction. It clearly differentiates itself from prior Hearst-pattern baselines and builds on Pasca (2015) in a non-trivial way (unsupervised property profiles + compositional scoring), so the technical idea is more than a simple combination of known tools. I deduct one point because the approach heavily depends on noisy SPO extractions (ReVerb/OLLIE) and appears incremental relative to its cited prior work without theoretical guarantees or released code to validate robustness, which lowers its novelty impact for a theory-first audience. | B(4): The paper presents a notable and practically useful idea—composing unsupervised modifier property-profile distributions to populate arbitrarily fine-grained IsA classes—which extends prior work (e.g., Pasca 2015) in a non-trivial, application-oriented way. The formulation of modifier semantics as learnable property profiles and their compositional use for multi-modifier class labels appears novel for downstream IsA extraction (authors claim first application of noun-compound interpretations here), and the reported >10 point AUC gain over a strong Hearst-pattern baseline supports that this is more than an incremental tweak. While it builds on existing fact-extraction and distributional techniques, the combination and target task represent a clear, differentiated contribution that would inform experts working on taxonomy induction and weakly supervised KB population.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a notable and coherent new perspective—composing learned modifier property-profile distributions to populate arbitrarily fine-grained IsA classes—which, according to the summary, is the first application of noun-compound interpretation to downstream IsA extraction. It clearly differentiates itself from prior Hearst-pattern baselines and builds on Pasca (2015) in a non-trivial way (unsupervised property profiles + compositional scoring), so the technical idea is more than a simple combination of known tools. I deduct one point because the approach heavily depends on noisy SPO extractions (ReVerb/OLLIE) and appears incremental relative to its cited prior work without theoretical guarantees or released code to validate robustness, which lowers its novelty impact for a theory-first audience.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a notable and practically useful idea—composing unsupervised modifier property-profile distributions to populate arbitrarily fine-grained IsA classes—which extends prior work (e.g., Pasca 2015) in a non-trivial, application-oriented way. The formulation of modifier semantics as learnable property profiles and their compositional use for multi-modifier class labels appears novel for downstream IsA extraction (authors claim first application of noun-compound interpretations here), and the reported >10 point AUC gain over a strong Hearst-pattern baseline supports that this is more than an incremental tweak. While it builds on existing fact-extraction and distributional techniques, the combination and target task represent a clear, differentiated contribution that would inform experts working on taxonomy induction and weakly supervised KB population.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early; the methodology is presented with formal elements (e.g., Eq.7) and related work coverage is thorough, and the writing quality is reported as good. From a theory-first perspective I note a few presentation gaps that prevent a perfect score: hyperparameters and experimental seeds are not reported and code is not mentioned (hurting reproducibility), and while assumptions are acknowledged they would benefit from clearer placement and discussion alongside the formal notation. Figures/tables and examples appear effective, but the paper would improve by explicitly documenting all experimental settings and making code/data available. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and presents a clear logical progression (intro → method → experiments → conclusion), with the main contributions and claims stated early (method for composing modifier semantics, unsupervised property-profile learning, and >10 point AUC gains). Notation appears consistent and equations are referenced (e.g., Eq.7) while related limitations and assumptions are explicitly discussed (Sect.4.4), which aids clarity. Minor presentation gaps remain—e.g., hyperparameter reporting is missing and code availability isn’t mentioned—which slightly reduce reproducibility-related clarity but do not hinder understanding of the core ideas.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early; the methodology is presented with formal elements (e.g., Eq.7) and related work coverage is thorough, and the writing quality is reported as good. From a theory-first perspective I note a few presentation gaps that prevent a perfect score: hyperparameters and experimental seeds are not reported and code is not mentioned (hurting reproducibility), and while assumptions are acknowledged they would benefit from clearer placement and discussion alongside the formal notation. Figures/tables and examples appear effective, but the paper would improve by explicitly documenting all experimental settings and making code/data available.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and presents a clear logical progression (intro → method → experiments → conclusion), with the main contributions and claims stated early (method for composing modifier semantics, unsupervised property-profile learning, and >10 point AUC gains). Notation appears consistent and equations are referenced (e.g., Eq.7) while related limitations and assumptions are explicitly discussed (Sect.4.4), which aids clarity. Minor presentation gaps remain—e.g., hyperparameter reporting is missing and code availability isn’t mentioned—which slightly reduce reproducibility-related clarity but do not hinder understanding of the core ideas.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important practical problem and demonstrates strong empirical gains (>10 AUC) with a novel, scalable approach that enables downstream noun‑compound interpretation for IsA extraction. Remaining concerns about dependence on noisy SPO extractions, limited robustness analyses, and incomplete reproducibility temper transformative impact but do not outweigh the clear empirical and methodological contribution, so a slightly above‑average impact score is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper attacks an important and practically useful problem—populating arbitrarily fine-grained IsA classes via modifier composition—and introduces a novel direction (composing learned modifier property-profiles) that plausibly shifts how researchers could approach fine-grained taxonomy induction. The >10-point AUC gains over a strong Hearst-pattern baseline and the claim to be first to apply noun-compound interpretations downstream suggest real empirical promise. However, the method heavily depends on noisy SPO extractions (ReVerb/OLLIE) without reported noise-robustness analyses, lacks released code/hyperparameters, and offers limited theoretical guarantees about when modifier composition will reliably reflect class membership; these issues constrain immediate uptake and broader impact. Overall, I view this as a solid, enabling contribution that opens new directions but not yet transformative given its dependency on noisy resources and limited reproducibility evidence.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important, practical problem—populating very fine-grained IsA classes without verbatim class mentions—using a scalable, weakly supervised approach that composes modifier semantics, which is directly useful for KB completion and taxonomy induction. The method is empirically compelling (reconstructing Wikipedia categories with >10 point AUC gain over a strong Hearst-based baseline) and relies on unsupervised property-profile learning from large SPO extractions, which practitioners can adopt without costly annotation. By operationalizing noun-compound interpretation for downstream IsA extraction it opens promising research directions for richer compositional extraction and KB expansion, though its impact may be tempered by dependence on the coverage/quality of extracted facts and the subsectivity assumption for modifiers.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important IsA/taxonomy induction problem, presents a scalable compositional method, and shows strong empirical gains versus a solid baseline. However, Reviewer A’s concerns about reliance on noisy OpenIE extractions and limited discussion of code/hyperparameter release and robustness reduce confidence in reproducibility and noise-resilience; for that reason I assign a slightly conservative fit score of 4 while acknowledging the work’s clear relevance and empirical contributions.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses IsA/taxonomy induction using modifier composition, which squarely fits the NLP/ML venues' scope and audience (fine-grained relation extraction, use of distributional resources and reranking). The problem formulation and evaluation (reconstructing Wikipedia categories, AUC against a strong Hearst-pattern baseline) follow community norms, but I have concerns about heavy reliance on noisy ReVerb/OLLIE extractions and lack of mentioned code/hyperparameter release and robustness analyses—issues that reduce reproducibility and theoretical guarantees expected at top venues. Overall the contribution is relevant and well-positioned, but reproducibility and noise-robustness shortcomings warrant only a slightly reduced fit score.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top-tier NLP/ML venue: it addresses a core information-extraction / taxonomy-population problem of clear interest to the community, proposes a practical, scalable method that leverages unsupervised property profiles and compositional modifier semantics, and demonstrates substantial empirical gains (>10 point AUC) on a realistic downstream task (Wikipedia category reconstruction). The methodology, use of large fact repositories and weak supervision, and evaluation via AUC against a strong Hearst-pattern baseline match the standards expected at the venue, and the work is positioned to appeal to both researchers and practitioners focused on KB population and fine-grained IsA extraction.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper delivers substantial empirical improvements against a strong baseline and is well-situated in related work, but both note missing reproducibility artifacts and limited analysis (ablation/sensitivity and robustness to noisy extractions). Given these consistent methodological gaps that weaken confidence in the proposed compositional semantics and its dependence on noisy fact sources, a moderate score is warranted. The score reflects solid empirical promise but insufficient analysis and reproducibility to justify a higher rating.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper reports substantial empirical gains and compares to a strong Hearst-pattern baseline, and it situates itself well in related work (coverage noted as thorough). However, from the summary there is no mention of formal theoretical modelling or guarantees for the proposed compositional semantics, limited or no ablation/sensitivity analyses (e.g., per-component ablations, effects of noisy ReVerb/OLLIE facts, or modifier interaction breakdowns), and key reproducibility artifacts (code, hyperparameters, seeds) are not provided. The authors do discuss some limitations (subsectivity assumption and dependence on fact quality), but they do not appear to perform noise-robustness or error analyses required by the paper’s reliance on noisy extractions, which keeps the evaluation from being fully comprehensive.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are strong on a practical, end-to-end task (reconstructing Wikipedia categories) and report substantial empirical gains (>10 point AUC) over a strong Hearst-pattern baseline, which supports the main claim about modifier-composition utility. The paper acknowledges important limitations (subsectivity, reliance on SPO fact coverage) and situates itself well in related work, but reproducibility is weakened by missing hyperparameter reporting and no mention of released code; it is also unclear from the summary whether thorough ablations/sensitivity analyses (e.g., ablation of ModsH/ModsI, impact of fact-repo quality) were performed. Overall this is solid empirical work with minor gaps in analysis and reproducibility that prevent a top score.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the empirical claim (>10 point AUC gain) is clear and plausible, and that the mathematical framing is reasonable. However, they also concur that key theoretical guarantees, robustness analyses to noisy SPO extractions, detailed experimental reporting (hyperparameters, significance), and code release are missing, limiting reproducibility and confidence in the conclusions. Given these substantive gaps, I score soundness/correctness as 3: promising and empirically supported but not yet fully justified or robustly validated.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by empirical evidence (notably a >10 point AUC gain over a strong Hearst-pattern baseline), but key theoretical and robustness analyses are missing. There are no formal proofs or guarantees for the proposed property-profile model and composition operators, and the method critically depends on noisy SPO extractions (ReVerb/OLLIE) without reported noise-robustness analysis, ablations, hyperparameter details, or released code to assess reproducibility. Given these gaps and the acknowledged but unquantified assumptions (modifier frequency correlates with important properties; subsectivity), the conclusions are promising but not yet fully justified by the presented technical evidence.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and appear to be supported by experiments showing >10 point AUC gains on a realistic Wikipedia category reconstruction task against a strong Hearst-pattern baseline. The mathematical framing (modifier-as-property-profile and compositional scoring using Eq.7) is plausible and no derivation errors are indicated in the summary, but important experimental details (hyperparameters, statistical significance tests) and code release are not reported, which weakens reproducibility. The authors acknowledge key assumptions (frequency indicates important properties; subsectivity) that introduce potential logical gaps in certain cases, but these are discussed and do not obviously overturn the empirical findings. Overall the methodology and results are sound with minor issues around experimental reporting and dependence on extraction quality.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper includes a strong, relevant Hearst-pattern baseline and reports meaningful AUC gains on the same task, so the main comparison is appropriate. However, they also concur that important competing approaches (e.g., embedding/distributional, supervised taxonomy/IsA methods and prior noun-compound work) are not directly compared and that implementation/hyperparameter details and code are missing, which limits fairness and reproducibility. Given these omissions, the empirical comparison is solid but incomplete, so a middle score of 3 is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a strong Hearst-pattern baseline and reports AUC improvements on the same Wikipedia reconstruction task, so there is at least one relevant, quantitative comparison using the same evaluation metric. However, only a Hearst-based baseline is mentioned—important competing approaches in taxonomy/IsA induction and distributional/unsupervised composition methods appear absent—so the set of baselines is incomplete. It is unclear whether setups fully control for shared resources (e.g., use of Hearst extractions as both baseline and resource O) or whether hyperparameters and seeds are aligned, which raises fairness concerns. As a theory-first reviewer, I also note the paper does not report code or hyperparameters, limiting reproducibility and confidence in the claimed superiority.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a strong, widely-used Hearst-pattern baseline on the same Wikipedia reconstruction task and reports AUC improvements (>10 points), so the main comparison is appropriate and evaluated under the same metric. However, based on the summary it appears important competing directions (e.g., embedding/distributional or supervised IsA/taxonomy-induction methods and prior noun-compound interpretation work) are not directly compared, and implementation/hyperparameter details (and code) are not reported to fully ensure fairness. The related-work discussion is thorough, but the empirical baselines list has minor omissions, so comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive.

main_claims: Presents a method to populate fine-grained classes (e.g., “1950s American jazz composers”) with instances by composing interpretations of individual modifiers relative to the head.

Models modifier semantics as property-profile distributions learned unsupervised (building on Pasca 2015) and uses these to score candidate instances, enabling IsA extraction for arbitrarily fine-grained class labels without requiring verbatim class occurrences in text.
Demonstrates empirical gains: reconstructing Wikipedia category pages yields >10 point AUC improvement over a strong baseline relying on widely-used Hearst patterns.
Claims to be the first work to apply noun-compound interpretations for downstream IsA relation extraction.
_summary.methodology: Learn a property profile for each modifier by mining SPO tuples from a large fact repository (facts extracted with ReVerb and OLLIE, and distributional similarity expansion), compute a scored membership function for instances via weighted matching of properties (Eq.7), combine modifier scores compositionally for multi-modifier class labels (ModsH/ModsI), and apply a weakly supervised logistic reranker trained on IsA pairs.
datasets: Fact repository D instantiated with extractions from ReVerb and OLLIE
baselines: Strong baseline relying on widely-used Hearst patterns
limitations: Assumes relations between a modifier M and head H that are discussed more often are more likely to capture important properties — authors note this assumption is imperfect (Section 4.4).
Assumes modifiers are subsective (acknowledged limitations per Kamp and Partee, 1995).
Method depends on the quality and coverage of extracted SPO facts and distributional similarity resources.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - IsA / taxonomy induction / information extraction

reviewer_a.name: Theory-First Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior NLP researcher with a PhD in computational linguistics and a track record of formal models for semantics and knowledge representation.
traits: Penalizes empirical-only papers that lack a clear formal statement of assumptions or theoretical guarantees (subtracts points if no formal model/proofs are provided)
Requires rigorous ablation and error analysis: deducts points if the paper omits per-component ablations and qualitative failure cases for main claims
Downweights results that rely heavily on noisy extractions (e.g., ReVerb/OLLIE) unless authors provide noise-robustness analysis or theoretical justification
Penalizes papers that do not release code, data, and experimental seeds for reproducibility
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I prioritize conceptual clarity and theoretical grounding; I judge papers mainly on whether they provide a clear formal model, assumptions, and analyses that explain why the method should work beyond empirical gains.

reviewer_b.name: Applied-Impact Empiricist
reviewer_b.background: Senior applied NLP researcher focusing on information extraction and knowledge base population with extensive experience evaluating practical systems on real-world corpora.
traits: Rewards substantial empirical improvement on practical tasks: grants higher scores for clear, consistent gains (e.g., >5–10 point AUC improvements) over strong baselines
Favors novel problem formulations and practical methods that enable extraction without verbatim class mentions (adds points for methods that expand coverage of KBs/classes)
Values weakly supervised and unsupervised approaches that scale without costly annotation: gives credit when methods use large unlabeled corpora and off-the-shelf extractors effectively
Prefers papers that demonstrate end-to-end utility (e.g., reconstructing Wikipedia categories) and provides error analysis focused on downstream behavior rather than formal guarantees
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I focus on the utility, scalability, and empirical gains of a method; I reward clear improvements on meaningful extraction problems and pragmatic solutions that can be adopted by practitioners.
